title: Vanilla RNN Limits
concept: Vanilla RNNs are simple but struggle to remember information for more than a few steps, which we'll explore next. Boss explain ends with: 'Next up: Backpropagation Through Time.'

exercises.prompt: What is the biggest challenge when using a 'Vanilla' RNN's biggest weakness?

options.text: They are too slow to run

exercises.explain: Vanilla RNNs are simple and easy to build, but they have trouble keeping track of information over long sequences.

cards.title: Short-Term Memory Problems
cards.body: Vanilla RNNs are great at looking at sequences of data, but they have a major flaw: they are very forgetful! If a sentence is too long, the beginning of the story gets lost in the mist. Let's find out why.

cards.title: What is a Vanilla RNN?
cards.body: A vanilla Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) is the simplest type of recurrent network. While it can process sequences, it has a short memory span. As new words or data arrive, the old information is quickly overwritten and forgotten.

cards.title: The Whisper Game
cards.body: Imagine trying to copy down a whisper passed along a circle of 20 people. By the time it reaches the last person, the message is completely scrambled. A vanilla RNN acts like this circle: it struggles to pass a message down a long chain without forgetting or messing up the original word.

cards.title: A Lost Subject
cards.body: If you feed the sentence 'The dog, which had fluffy brown fur and loved to chase squirrels in the yard, barked' into a vanilla RNN, the network might forget the word 'dog' by the time it needs to predict the action 'barked' because there were too many words in between.

cards.title: Myth: Infinite Memory
cards.body: You might think that because RNNs have a 'hidden state' (memory), they can remember things forever. In reality, vanilla RNNs can only hold onto information for a few steps before it completely fades away.

cards.title: Memory Retention Limit
cards.body: Vanilla RNNs keep information for short trips, but fail on long-distance journeys.

left.text: RNN remembers the beginning and predicts the end correctly.

right.text: Early words fade away; predictions at the end become random guesses.

exercises.prompt: What is the main limitation of a vanilla RNN?

options.text: They cannot process text at all.
They struggle to remember things from many steps ago.
They are too fast to read the input.
They only work with numbers, not words.

exercises.explain: Vanilla RNNs process information step-by-step, and because they overwrite their memory at each step, early information is lost over long sequences.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Vanilla RNNs are the best choice for memorizing entire books.
exercises.explain: Vanilla RNNs have a short memory capacity and are not suited for long-term dependencies.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the statement about simple recurrent networks.
exercises.sentence: A simple ________ RNN struggles to remember information for more than a few steps.
exercises.explain: Vanilla RNNs have a short-term memory limit, which makes them struggle with long sentences or sequences.

exercises.prompt: Which of the following represents an incorrect claim about vanilla RNNs?

options.text: An AI engineer says: 'We need a different model to translate long books.'
An AI bot says: 'I use a vanilla RNN to perfectly remember the first chapter of a novel.'
A student says: 'A vanilla RNN works fine for predicting the very next word in a short phrase.'

exercises.explain: AI models that use vanilla RNNs get confused by long sentences because their memory fades quickly. The other options show correct understanding.

exercises.prompt: If you give a vanilla RNN a very long paragraph, what happens to the words at the very beginning?

options.text: The early words fade away and get lost.
The RNN runs out of computer memory.
The RNN reads the sentence backwards.
The output words become twice as long.

exercises.explain: Because vanilla RNNs struggle with long-term memory, we need a special way to train them and update their weights over time steps. Next up: Backpropagation Through Time.
